coredata.dc:title: Bright carbon dots via inner filter effect for the sensitive determination of the purine metabolic disorder in human fluids
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Ju
Nan, Qian
Yang, Jing
Yang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the high performance fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized with maleic acid, tris and benzoic acid as raw materials by one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained carbon dots with uniform size emitted strong blue fluorescence, which the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 250 nm and 415 nm, respectively. Under the optimum condition, it was meaningfully founded that the reaction between the carbon dots and uric acid resulting in the fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots at the emission spectrum of 415 nm. The reason was that they had a synergistic effect between the fluorescence internal filtering effect and the static quenching effect. The fluorescence internal filter effect sensing system was constructed by using uric acid as the absorbable material and carbon dots as the luminophore. Hence, a fluorescence quenching method for the determination of uric acid was established in the concentration range from 5.0 to 400 μM with the detection limit (3σ/S) of 2.26 μM. Thus, a fluorescent sensing assay for the determination of uric acid was founded and confirmed in human fluids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Fluorescence quenching
Uric acid detection
Inner filter effect
The analysis of real examples

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Uric Acid in Human Fluids
Selective Analysis of the Fluorescent Probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and size distribution histogram (B) of MCDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of MCDs aqueous solution excited from 220 to 290 nm. (B) The UV–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of MCDs. Inset: the photographs of MCDs under visible light (I) and 365 nm UV light (II).
FT-IR spectra of the synthetic MCDs.
The fluorescence response of the obtained MCDs respond for different pH values (A), and reaction time (B).

(A) The selectivity of the gotten MCDs toward uric acid and other interferences at pH 6.0. (B) The selective of the fluorescence probe for the presence of above materials (conditions: pH 6.0; uric acid: 200 μM). Uric acid: UA, histidine: His, phenylalanine: Phe, glutathione: GSH, lipoic acid: LA, l-carnitine: LC, semicarbazide hydrochloride: SH, amikacin sulfate: AS, sisomicin: Sis, p-toluenesulfonic acid: TsOH, ascorbic acid: AA, penicillamine: Pen.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of different amounts of uric acid. (B) The relationship between the fluorescent intensity and uric acid concentration. Calibration graph for the UA determination in the range of 5-70 μM (C), and 100–416 μM (D).

(A) UV–vis spectra of UA (curve a), the excitation spectra of MCDs (curve b). (B) The fluorescence decay curves of MCDs and MCDs-UA. (C) Stern–Volmer curves of the MCDs–UA solution system under different temperature condition.
Illustration shows preparation process of fluorescent MCDs and detection principle for UA. UA: uric acid.
The some proposed methods for determining UA are compared.
Determination of UA in human urine samples (1–3) and serum samples (4–5).

head.ce:title: Bright carbon dots via inner filter effect for the sensitive determination of the purine metabolic disorder in human fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the high performance fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized with maleic acid, tris and benzoic acid as raw materials by one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained carbon dots with uniform size emitted strong blue fluorescence, which the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 250 nm and 415 nm, respectively. Under the optimum condition, it was meaningfully founded that the reaction between the carbon dots and uric acid resulting in the fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots at the emission spectrum of 415 nm. The reason was that they had a synergistic effect between the fluorescence internal filtering effect and the static quenching effect. The fluorescence internal filter effect sensing system was constructed by using uric acid as the absorbable material and carbon dots as the luminophore. Hence, a fluorescence quenching method for the determination of uric acid was established in the concentration range from 5.0 to 400 μM with the detection limit (3σ/S) of 2.26 μM. Thus, a fluorescent sensing assay for the determination of uric acid was founded and confirmed in human fluids.

Illustration shows preparation process of fluorescent MCDs and detection principle for UA. UA: uric acid.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (2,6,8-trihydroxypurine, UA), as a very important biological molecule, is the ultima product of purine metabolism [1,2]. In a healthy person's body, the most of uric acid is filtered out through the kidneys and excreted mainly in the form of urine [3]. Thus, the concentration of uric acid can reflect whether the body's mechanism is normal [4]. The normal concentration of uric acid maintains in the range from 0.24 to 0.52 mM in serum and 1.4 to 4.5 mM in urine [5–7]. High concentration levels of uric acid can cause many diseases, such as hyperuricemia, gout, kidney diseases, physiological disorders and Lesch-Nyhan syndrome [8–10]. While, low concentration levels of uric acid can also bring about Wilson disease [11]. Accordingly, the detection of uric acid in human body fluids is very necessary, which is the basis of clinical diagnosis of related diseases. The methods of detecting uric acid include fluorescence [12], HPLC [13], enzyme [14], electrochemical method [15], chemiluminescence [16], etc. Recent years, the fluorescence method has been getting more attention due to their low cost, simple operation and fast response.

Carbon nanotubes were accidentally discovered in the process of single-walled separation and purification, whose size is less than 10 nm [17,18]. And carbon dots have the following advantages: stability, water solubility and low toxicity [19]. Fluorescent carbon dots have been widely used in a variety of analytes, such as pH detection [20], cation detection [21], anion detection [22], the other molecule detection [23], photocatalytic application [24], biomedical application [25], and other application [26].

Lately, the principle of partial fluorescence quenching is the internal filtering effect (IFE) [27–29]. The fluorescence internal filtering effect refers to this phenomenon that the absorption spectrum of the absorbent substance and the excitation spectrum or/and the emission spectrum of the fluorescent substance have a considerable degree of spectral overlap. The fluorescent internal filter effect can convert the light absorption signal of the light absorbing body into the fluorescence signal, thereby improving the sensitivity of the detection method [30].

In this research, we found a handy and sensitive carbon dots (MCDs) sensor for determining UA concentration value. According to the principle of fluorescence internal filter effect, the fluorescent sensing system is constructed with uric acid as the light absorber and the carbon dots as the luminophore. A fluorescence quenching method is established for the determination of uric acid in human fluids (Scheme 1 ). The method is simple, sensitive and selective, which has been successfully applied in the analysis of real examples.

A transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, America) was applied to inspect the size and morphology of the MCDs. All fluorescence spectra were obtained on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Absorption spectra were determined using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was got from the FTIR-8400S Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan). The pH value of the aqueous solutions was recorded with a PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai Scientific Instruments Company, China). The carbon dots solution was obtained by a DHG-9245A electrically heated air blast constant temperature drying oven (Shanghai Qi Xin Scientific Instruments Company, China).

Maleic acid was obtained from Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Benzoic acid was purchased from Wuhan Institute of Organic Synthetic Materials (Wuhan, china). Tris was received from Hongkong Farco Chemical Supplies (Hongkong, China). Uric acid was gained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Procurement Supply Reagent Factory (Shanghai, China). Britton–Robinson buffer solution: H3PO4, H3BO3 and CH3COOH (the concentrations of the three acids were 0.04 M) were mixed with 0.2 M NaOH solution in a proportion. The experimental water was ultrapure water, and the all reagents were analytical pure.

In a typical procedure for synthesis of MCDs: maleic acid (1 g), tris (1 g) and benzoic acid (1 g) were dissolved in ultrapure water (25 mL) with stirring. The solution was transferred into a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated in an electrically heated air blast constant temperature drying oven at 250 °C for 6 h. The product was cooled to room temperature, and the brown-yellow fluorescent carbon dots solution was obtained. The got solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min to discard precipitation, then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble substance. The obtained solution was dialyzed for 24 h (change water every 6 h). Finally, the gained MCDs solution was stored at 4 °C.

In a typical analysis, 200 μL of carbon dots solution was added to 200 μL of BR buffer solution (pH = 6). After that, 200 μL of uric acid with different concentrations was added in the system and mixed completely at room temperature for 1 min. Next, the corresponding fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was recorded at the emission spectrum of 415 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Uric Acid in Human Fluids
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of UA was tested with a standard addition method. The human plasma samples were obtained from different healthy people in local hospital. Human serum samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The obtained supernatant liquid was not additional treatment. Fresh urine samples were provided by healthy crowd. The samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to precipitate the proteins. Then they were diluted 50 times with phosphate buffer saline (0.2 mol/L, pH = 7.0) without other pretreatments. 200 μL of the treated samples were used to follow the same procedure for UA detection.

The carbon dots were obtained by hydrothermal route using maleic acid, tris and benzoic acid as raw materials. A reference method [31] was used to measure the fluorescence quantum yield of the synthetic MCDs, and the corresponding value was 17.25%. The size and morphology of MCDs were observed with a transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1A, we can see that the prepared MCDs have a uniform dispersion, which is the proof for the formation of carbon dots. The size distribution of the obtained MCDs is mainly in the range from 2 to 5 nm (Fig. 1B). And the average diameter is about 3.83 nm by statistical analysis of more than 100 particles.

The optical properties of MCDs were recorded by fluorescence spectra and UV–visible absorption spectra. We studied the fluorescence emission spectra of MCDs at various excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 2A, the corresponding fluorescence emission intensity increase and then gradually become weaker with the excitation wavelength from 220 nm to 290 nm. Moreover, when the excitation wavelength at 250 nm, the corresponding emission intensity is maximum. There are the absorption spectrum, the maximum excitation and emission spectrum of MCDs in Fig. 2B. It can be clearly observed that the absorption band of the acquired MCDs is broad instead of absorption peak, which may be related to raw materials of the synthesis of carbon dots. Further, the fluorescence peak of the excitation spectrum at 250 nm is significantly higher than that at 326 nm. Furthermore, The MCDs solution shows obvious blue fluorescence under UV light and presents colorless and transparent in the sunlight (inset Fig. 2B).

The chemical bonds on the surface of the obtained MCDs were analysed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the MCDs have characteristic absorption bands with NH and OH stretching vibration at 3439.16 cm−1. The peaks at 2979.47 and 2892.85 cm−1 are due to the CH stretching vibration [32]. The peak at 1637.98 cm−1 is belongs to stretching vibration of CO [33]. The stretching vibration of CN is showed by the peak at 1386.42 cm−1 [34]. The peak at 1096.80 cm−1 is considered to be CO stretching vibration absorption.
To gain the high effective response of the carbon dots to uric acid, the reaction time and the pH value in solution were chosen as the important experimental elements [35].

To optimize the effect of acidity, different pH value of Britton-Robinson buffer was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of MCDs is dependent on the acidity to a certain extent. With the pH value in a range from 5.0 to 9.0, the fluorescence intensity increase and then gradually decrease. When the intensity is the maximum, the corresponding optimum pH is 6. Therefore, the pH value is 6 as the best pH for the detecting system.

Next, the impact of reaction time on the test was taken into account. By adding 200 μL of 10−3 M uric acid into the MCDs solution, the corresponding fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly within 1 min and remained almost stable for the next 50 min (Fig. 4B). It meant that UA could be quickly detected by this method without strict time control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective Analysis of the Fluorescent Probe
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is another important factor in uric acid testing, which has been evaluated through a series of experimentations. As shown in Fig. 5A, the interfering substances have uric acid, CdCl2, Cocl2, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, histidine, phenylalanine, glutathione, lipoic acid, l-carnitine, semicarbazide hydrochloride, amikacin sulfate, sisomicin, p-toluenesulfonic acid, ascorbic acid, penicillamine, respectively. The penicillamine concentration was 0.84 mg/mL, and the concentration of the rest substances were 200 μM. The result implied that the fluorescence quenching was noticed after adding uric acid, and effect of the other substances could be almost negligible. The UA was mixed with the MCDs, then the aforementioned interference was added to the mixed solution. As shown in Fig. 5B, this suggests that the efficient fluorescence quenching is due to the reaction of carbon dots and uric acid.

The fluorescence intensity of MCDs gradually decreased with the increase of uric acid concentration (Fig. 6A). As shown in Fig. 6B, the MCDs and uric acid concentration have a certain relationship. When the concentration of uric acid is 5–70 μM, the relationship could be presented by the following equations: ΔF = 1.80C + 8.11 (Fig. 6C). The fluorescent decrement can be counted as: ΔF = F − F 0, where F and F 0 represent the fluorescence of mixed and reagent solution, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6D, when the concentration of uric acid is 100–416 μM, the relationship follows this equation: ΔF = 1.02C + 95.01. In addition, the detection limit and dynamic range of the proposed fluorescent probe are similar with previously reported methods for detecting uric acid in Table 1 , and the fluorescence quenching method is faster and simpler.

Compared with the fluorescence resonance energy transfer method, the fluorescence internal filtration effect has no the covalent bonds between absorbents and fluorophores [36]. So, the fluorescent probe is easy to synthesize. The IFE occurs if the absorption band of substance has overlap with the excitation and/or emission peak of the fluorophore to some extent.

As mentioned above, the choice of the fluorescent probe is interrelated with the appropriate absorbents and fluorophores. Fortunately, we found a way to detect UA using carbon dots by this principle. Fluorescence and ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy were used to explore the excitation and emission spectra of MCDs and the absorption spectra of uric acid, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7A, there are two peaks at the wavelength of 200 to 500 nm in the excitation spectrum of MCDs, and the maximum excitation peak is located at 250 nm. The strong absorption peak of uric acid can also be observed at 200–300 nm. It can be seen that the excitation spectrum of MCDs partially overlaps with the absorption spectrum of uric acid. In a word, the detection mechanism belongs to the fluorescence internal filtering effect.

Furthermore, the quenching mechanism is also divided into static quenching and dynamic quenching. The fluorescence decay and the Stern–Volmer equation can be considered to research the reaction mechanism. Fluorescence lifetime is a crucial parameter for judging static and dynamic quenching. If it is dynamic quenching, the presence of the quencher reduces the fluorescence lifetime, τ 0 / τ = F 0 / F. But the static quencher process does not change the fluorescence lifetime, τ 0 / τ = 1. As shown in Fig. 7B, the fluorescence lifetime value is almost invariable after adding uric acid. Therefore, this reaction system (MCDs-UA) belongs to the static quenching effect (SQE).

The Stern-Volmer equation is another key parameter to distinguish dynamic quenching or static quenching. This reaction system was studied by using the Stern-Volmer equation under two temperature conditions (Fig. 7C). The Stern-Volmer curve equation: F 0 / F = 1 + Ksv[Q], where F 0 and F indicate the fluorescence intensity of MCDs and MCDs-UA solution, respectively. Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching rate constant, [Q] represents the concentration of uric acid. The results show that the quenching constant decreases with the augment of temperature, which expresses the reaction system is a static quenching process [37].
In summary, the quenching of the MCDs-UA probe was the synergistic effect of IFE and SQE.

In order to validate the applicability of the proposed method, the human fluids samples were determined using the standard addition method. The result shown in Table 2 , the corresponding recovery value of UA in plasma and urine samples are 91.10–103.9%, 96.77–103.44%, respectively. And the relative standard deviations (RSD) of samples are 2.40–4.46%, 1.30–3.52%, respectively. This analytical strategy shows that applicability of the method is acceptable and can be applied to the determination of uric acid in the real analysis.

In this study, we had successfully obtained MCDs by using simple hydrothermal treatment with maleic acid, tris and benzoic acid as raw materials. The size of the water-soluble MCDs ranged from 2 to 5 nm with an average size of 3.83 nm, emitting strong blue fluorescence. After the addition of UA, the fluorescence intensity of MCDs was effectively quenched. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the reaction system was attributed to the combination of IFE and SQE. Thus, a simple fluorescence sensing system was put forward to detect uric acid rapidly and sensitively by using MCDs as the fluorescence probe. Moreover, there was no need for any fluorescent modification in this study. The method has good practicability and has been successfully applied to the determination of UA in real samples, providing a method for clinical diagnosis in the future.
